words.m: verb present active participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: Paul and Timothy, bondservants of Christ Jesus, to all the holy ones in Christ Jesus who are in Philippi, together with the overseers and deacons:
verses.note: GREEK: "Bondservants" (δοῦλοι, douloi, G1401) denotes slaves—those wholly owned by a master. Paul deliberately places himself and Timothy in this subordinate role to Christ Jesus. "Overseers" (ἐπισκόποις, episkopois, G1985) and "deacons" (διακόνοις, diakonois, G1249) represent the earliest attested dual structure of local church leadership. HISTORICAL: Philippi was a Roman colonia in Macedonia, where Paul founded the first European church (Acts 16:12-40). The mention of structured offices suggests a maturing congregation approximately ten years after its founding.

verses.flow: Grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Grace" (χάρις, charis) and "peace" (εἰρήνη, eirēnē) combine Greco-Roman and Hebrew (shalom) greeting forms, signifying the unification of Jew and Gentile in one new humanity. Paul coordinates God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ as co-source of divine grace and peace, placing the Lord Jesus in a position of functional equality with the Father (cf. 2 Cor 13:14).

verses.flow: I give thanks to my God upon every remembrance of you,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: always in every petition of mine on behalf of you all, making the petition with joy,
because of your fellowship in the gospel from the first day until now,
verses.note: GREEK: "Fellowship" (κοινωνίᾳ, koinōnia, G2842) denotes active partnership and participation, not mere association. The Philippians' koinōnia "in the gospel" (εἰς τὸ εὐαγγέλιον) includes both spiritual solidarity and material financial support (cf. Phil 4:15-16). HISTORICAL: The Philippians were the only church that entered into a giving-and-receiving partnership with Paul from the beginning of his mission work.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: being confident of this very thing, that the one having begun a good work in you will bring it to completion until the day of Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "good work" (ἔργον ἀγαθόν) begun by God himself assures the believer of divine perseverance unto completion. The "day of Christ Jesus" (ἡμέρας Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ) is an eschatological reference to the return and final appearing of Christ (cf. 1 Cor 1:8; 2 Cor 1:14). GREEK: "Began" (ἐναρξάμενος, enarxamenos, G1728) is a rare term used in sacrificial contexts for the inauguration of a sacred rite, suggesting God's work in believers has a consecrated, covenantal character.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Just as it is right for me to think this on behalf of you all, because I have you in my heart, both in my chains and in the defense and confirmation of the gospel, you all being fellow-partakers with me of grace.
For God is my witness, how I long for you all in the affections of Christ Jesus.
And this I pray, that your love may abound yet more and more in full knowledge and all discernment,

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: so that you may approve the things that are excellent, that you may be sincere and without offense unto the day of Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Approve the things that are excellent" (δοκιμάζειν τὰ διαφέροντα) may also be rendered "test the things that differ"—the verb δοκιμάζω (G1381) denotes assaying metals for genuineness. "Sincere" (εἰλικρινεῖς, eilikrineis, G1506) etymologically suggests "judged by sunlight," that is, tested by the light and found pure. THEOLOGICAL: Moral discernment is here presented not as mere human wisdom but as a fruit of love abounding in knowledge—an epistemology rooted in sanctified affection.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having been filled with the fruit of righteousness which is through Jesus Christ, unto the glory and praise of God.
Now I want you to know, brothers, that the things concerning me have come rather unto the advancement of the gospel,
so that my chains have become manifest in Christ in the whole praetorium and to all the rest,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "praetorium" (πραιτωρίῳ, praitōriō, G4232) likely refers to the Praetorian Guard—the imperial bodyguard stationed in Rome—or to the governor's residence if Paul was imprisoned elsewhere. Either way, Paul's chains became an occasion for the gospel to penetrate the highest levels of Roman imperial power (cf. Phil 4:22). GREEK: The Latin loanword praitōrion entered Greek through Roman military administration.

words.m: verb perfect active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: and the greater number of the brothers in the Lord, having been made confident by my chains, dare more abundantly to speak the word without fear.
Some indeed even out of envy and strife, but some also out of good will, proclaim Christ.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: The ones on the one hand do so out of love, knowing that I am appointed for the defense of the gospel;

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: but the others out of selfish ambition proclaim Christ, not sincerely, supposing to raise up affliction in my chains.
What then? Only that in every way, whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is proclaimed, and in this I rejoice; yes, and I will rejoice.
For I know that this will turn out for my deliverance through your petition and the supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The supply of the Spirit of Jesus Christ" (ἐπιχορηγίας τοῦ πνεύματος Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ) is a remarkable title identifying the Holy Spirit as belonging to and proceeding from Jesus Christ (cf. Rom 8:9; Gal 4:6). GREEK: "Supply" (ἐπιχορηγία, epichorēgia, G2024) originally denoted the lavish funding of a chorus by a wealthy patron—implying the Spirit is given abundantly, not sparingly.

verses.flow: according to my earnest expectation and hope, that in nothing I shall be put to shame, but in all boldness, as always, also now Christ shall be magnified in my body, whether through life or through death.
For to me, to live is Christ, and to die is gain.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is one of the most compressed christological statements in the Pauline corpus. "To live is Christ" (τὸ ζῆν Χριστός) identifies Christ not merely as the reason for living but as the very content and substance of life itself. Death becomes "gain" (κέρδος, kerdos, G2771) only because it brings unmediated presence with Christ (v. 23). This verse demolishes any framework that separates existence from christological orientation.

verses.flow: But if to live in the flesh—this means fruit of labor for me—then what I shall choose I do not make known.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For I am hard pressed out of the two, having the desire unto departing and being with Christ, for it is very much more better;
verses.note: GREEK: "Hard pressed" (συνέχομαι, synechomai, G4912) means to be constrained or hemmed in from both sides. "Departing" (ἀναλῦσαι, analysai, G360) is a metaphor drawn from loosing a ship from its moorings or striking a military camp—a vivid image of death as departure toward Christ. "Very much more better" (πολλῷ μᾶλλον κρεῖσσον) stacks three comparative expressions, an unusual Greek intensification revealing the depth of Paul's longing.

verses.flow: yet to remain in the flesh is more necessary on account of you.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: And being confident of this, I know that I shall remain and continue with you all, unto your advancement and joy of the faith,
so that your boasting may abound in Christ Jesus in me, through my coming to you again.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Only conduct yourselves worthily of the gospel of Christ, so that whether having come and having seen you, or being absent, I may hear the things concerning you: that you stand firm in one spirit, with one soul striving together for the faith of the gospel,
verses.note: GREEK: "Conduct yourselves worthily" (πολιτεύεσθε, politeuesthe, G4176) is a politically charged verb meaning "live as citizens." In the Roman colonia of Philippi, where citizens prized their Roman politeia, Paul redirects civic identity toward the heavenly commonwealth (cf. Phil 3:20). THEOLOGICAL: The unity of "one spirit" (ἑνὶ πνεύματι) and "one soul" (μιᾷ ψυχῇ) likely distinguishes the Holy Spirit's enabling from the believers' united inner resolve. "Striving together" (συναθλοῦντες, G4866) is an athletic metaphor—competing side by side as teammates.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: and not being frightened in anything by the adversaries—which is to them a demonstration of destruction, but of your salvation, and this from God—
because to you it has been graciously granted on behalf of Christ, not only to believe in him, but also to suffer on behalf of him,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Suffering is here presented as a gracious gift (ἐχαρίσθη, from χαρίζομαι, G5483—cognate with χάρις, "grace") granted on behalf of Christ. Both faith and suffering are parallel gifts from God, placing persecution within the economy of divine grace rather than outside it. This reframes the Philippians' affliction as a mark of divine favor, not divine abandonment (cf. Acts 5:41; 1 Pet 4:12-14).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: having the same struggle such as you saw in me, and now hear to be in me.
If therefore there is any encouragement in Christ, if any consolation of love, if any fellowship of the Spirit, if any affections and compassions,
verses.note: GREEK: παράκλησις (paraklēsis, Strong's G3874) means "encouragement" or "exhortation," from the same root as παράκλητος (paraklētos), the title given to the Holy Spirit in John 14:16. The fourfold "if" (εἴ τις) structure is a first-class conditional in Greek, assuming the reality of each condition — Paul is not expressing doubt but appealing to what they already experience in Christ. σπλάγχνα (splanchna, G4698) refers to the inner organs regarded as the seat of deep emotion.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: fulfill my joy, that you might think the same thing, having the same love, united in soul, thinking the one thing,
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Paul employs four parallel phrases to define the unity he desires: "think the same thing," "having the same love," "united in soul," "thinking the one thing." The verb φρονέω (phroneō, G5426) — to think, to set one's mind — appears twice, framing the exhortation and forming a key word throughout the chapter (vv. 2, 3, 5).

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: nothing according to selfish ambition nor according to vain-glory, but in humility of mind esteeming one another as surpassing yourselves,
verses.note: GREEK: ἐριθεία (eritheia, G2052) originally denoted a day-laborer's self-seeking work for hire; by Paul's usage it means factious, selfish ambition. κενοδοξία (kenodoxia, G2754) is a compound of κενός ("empty") and δόξα ("glory") — literally "empty glory." ταπεινοφροσύνη (tapeinophrosunē, G5012) — "humility of mind" — was considered a vice in Greco-Roman culture but is here elevated as a Christian virtue.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: not each looking to the things of yourselves, but each also to the things of others.
Think this in yourselves which was also in Christ Jesus,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse introduces the great Christ-hymn (vv. 5-11). The imperative φρονεῖτε (phroneite) calls the Philippians to adopt the very mindset (φρόνησις) of Christ Jesus himself, establishing the incarnation not merely as theology but as ethical pattern for the community.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: who, existing in the form of God, did not consider equality with God a thing to be grasped,
verses.note: GREEK: μορφή (morphē, G3444) signifies the essential form or nature — not mere outward appearance (σχῆμα). Christ existing (ὑπάρχων, a present participle indicating continuous prior existence) in the form of God means he possessed the very nature of deity. ἁρπαγμός (harpagmos, G725) is a rare term (near hapax in NT) debated as either "something seized" or "something to be exploited." The sense is that Christ did not regard his divine equality as something to be clutched or used for self-advantage. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The assertion of equality (ἴσα) with God places the pre-incarnate Christ at the supreme position of the divine council, co-equal with the Father before all created spiritual beings.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: but emptied himself, having taken the form of a bondservant, having come to be in the likeness of men; and having been found in appearance as a man,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: ἐκένωσεν (ekenōsen, from κενόω, G2758) — "he emptied himself" — is the foundation of kenosis theology. This does not imply a loss of divine nature but a voluntary relinquishment of divine prerogatives. The taking of δούλου μορφήν ("the form of a bondservant") is a deliberate descent from the highest station to the lowest. TYPOLOGY: The "form of a bondservant" echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 52:13-53:12 (LXX παῖς/δοῦλος). Christ fulfills the Isaianic Servant who takes on affliction for the sake of others.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: he humbled himself, having become obedient unto death — and death of a cross.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The descent reaches its nadir: self-humbling, obedience unto death, and specifically σταυροῦ (stauros) — death of a cross, the most shameful Roman execution reserved for slaves and insurrectionists. The word δέ introduces "death of a cross" as a shocking intensification. HISTORICAL: Crucifixion was so despised that Cicero called it "the most cruel and disgusting penalty" (In Verrem 2.5.165). For a Roman colony like Philippi, this language carried maximum rhetorical force.

verses.flow: Therefore also God highly exalted him and granted to him the name that is above every name,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: διό ("therefore") marks the turning point — the exaltation answers the humiliation. ὑπερύψωσεν (hyperypsoō, G5251) appears only here in the NT, meaning "super-exalted" or "exalted to the highest degree." God grants (ἐχαρίσατο, from χαρίζομαι — a gracious bestowal) the name above every name. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The granting of the supreme name (τὸ ὄνομα τὸ ὑπὲρ πᾶν ὄνομα) implies enthronement over all heavenly powers, principalities, and every member of the divine council — a cosmic investiture of authority (cf. Eph 1:20-21).

verses.flow: that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow, of those in heaven and those on earth and those under the earth,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This is a direct echo of Isaiah 45:23 (LXX), where YHWH declares that every knee shall bow to him alone. Paul applies this text — a monotheistic declaration of YHWH's sole sovereignty — directly to Jesus. The three-tiered cosmos (ἐπουρανίων, ἐπιγείων, καταχθονίων — heavenly, earthly, and under-the-earth beings) encompasses all spiritual and physical reality, including angelic beings, the living, and the dead.

verses.flow: and every tongue should confess that Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The confession κύριος Ἰησοῦς Χριστός ("Jesus Christ is Lord") is likely the earliest Christian creedal formula. That this universal confession redounds εἰς δόξαν θεοῦ πατρός ("to the glory of God the Father") shows that Christ's lordship does not compete with but rather fulfills the Father's purpose. GREEK: ἐξομολογήσηται (exomologēsetai, G1843) means "to openly and gladly confess" — not a forced admission but an acknowledgment of reality.

verses.flow: So then, my beloved, just as you have always obeyed, not as in my presence only, but now much more in my absence, with fear and trembling work out your own salvation;

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: for it is God who is working in you both the willing and the working for the sake of his good pleasure.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse provides the theological ground for verse 12. The imperative to "work out" salvation is not self-reliance; it is empowered by God's sovereign working (ἐνεργῶν, energōn, G1754 — from which English "energy" derives). God is the one producing both τὸ θέλειν ("the willing") and τὸ ἐνεργεῖν ("the working"), revealing that divine sovereignty and human responsibility operate in concert, not opposition. εὐδοκίας (eudokias, G2107) — "good pleasure" — emphasizes God's gracious, sovereign delight as the motive.

verses.flow: Do all things without grumblings and disputings,

words.m: verb perfect passive participle genitive singular feminine

verses.flow: that you may become blameless and pure, children of God without blemish in the midst of a crooked and perverted generation, among whom you shine as luminaries in the world,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The phrase γενεᾶς σκολιᾶς καὶ διεστραμμένης ("a crooked and perverted generation") directly echoes Deuteronomy 32:5 (LXX), from the Song of Moses, where Israel is rebuked for unfaithfulness. Paul applies this to the pagan world surrounding the Philippians, casting the church as the true faithful children of God. φωστῆρες (phōstēres, G5458) means "luminaries" or "stars" — used in the LXX of Genesis 1:14-16 for the heavenly lights, elevating believers to a cosmic witness role.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: holding fast the word of life, for a boast to me unto the day of Christ, that I did not run in vain nor labor in vain.
But even if I am being poured out as a drink offering upon the sacrifice and priestly service of your faith, I rejoice and rejoice together with you all.
verses.note: GREEK: σπένδομαι (spendomai, G4689) — "I am being poured out as a drink offering" — is a sacrificial term used in the LXX for the wine libation poured over a burnt offering (Num 15:5-7). Paul views his potential martyrdom as a secondary offering poured upon the primary sacrifice — which is the Philippians' own faith (θυσίᾳ καὶ λειτουργίᾳ τῆς πίστεως ὑμῶν). λειτουργία (leitourgia, G3009) is a priestly/liturgical term, framing their faith as a sacred offering to God.

verses.flow: And in the same way also you rejoice and rejoice together with me.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I hope in the Lord Jesus to send Timothy to you soon, that I also may be of good courage, having come to know the things concerning you.
For I have no one of equal soul, who will genuinely care for the things concerning you.
For they all seek the things of themselves, not the things of Jesus Christ.
But you know his proven character, that as a child with a father, he served with me for the gospel.
Him therefore I hope to send as soon as I see how things go with me;
but I trust in the Lord that I myself also shall come soon.
But I considered it necessary to send to you Epaphroditus, my brother and fellow-worker and fellow-soldier, and your messenger and minister to my need,
verses.note: GREEK: Paul stacks five titles for Epaphroditus: ἀδελφόν (brother), συνεργόν (fellow-worker), συστρατιώτην (fellow-soldier) — relating to Paul; then ἀπόστολον (apostle/messenger) and λειτουργόν (minister/liturgist) — relating to the Philippians. ἀπόστολος here carries its broader sense of "sent one" or "emissary" rather than the technical apostolic office. HISTORICAL: Epaphroditus was the Philippians' delegate who brought their financial gift to Paul in prison (Phil 4:18) and nearly died serving him.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: since he was longing for you all and was deeply distressed because you heard that he was sick.
For indeed he was sick, near unto death; but God had mercy on him, and not on him only but also on me, that I might not have sorrow upon sorrow.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Therefore I sent him the more eagerly, that having seen him again you may rejoice, and I may be less sorrowful.
Receive him therefore in the Lord with all joy, and hold such ones in honor,

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: because on account of the work of Christ he drew near unto death, having risked his life, that he might fill up your lack of service toward me.
verses.note: GREEK: παραβολευσάμενος (paraboleusamenos) — "having risked" — is an exceptionally rare word, a gambling term meaning "having staked" or "having hazarded" his life. Some manuscripts read παραβουλευσάμενος. The term evokes the reckless courage of one who throws the dice with his own existence. THEOLOGICAL: Epaphroditus's near-death service to Paul is presented as filling up (ἀναπληρώσῃ) what the Philippian congregation could not do in person — an act of representative, vicarious ministry that mirrors in a lesser way the self-giving of Christ in the hymn above (vv. 6-8).

verses.flow: Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you, to me indeed is not troublesome, but for you is safeguarding.
Beware of the dogs, beware of the evil workers, beware of the mutilation.
verses.note: GREEK: "Kynас" (κύνας, dogs) was a term of contempt applied to those outside the covenant. "Katatomēn" (κατατομήν, mutilation) is a sharp wordplay on "peritomē" (circumcision) in v.3 — Paul deliberately uses the prefix κατα- (down/against) to mock those demanding circumcision of Gentile believers, reducing their rite to mere cutting of flesh. THEOLOGICAL: The threefold "blépete" (beware/look out) creates an urgent rhetorical triplet warning against Judaizing opponents.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine
verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we are the circumcision, who by the Spirit of God worship, and boast in Christ Jesus, and have not put confidence in flesh,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul radically redefines circumcision: the true "peritomē" (περιτομή) is not physical but spiritual — those who worship by the Spirit of God. This echoes Deut 30:6 and Jer 4:4 where the prophets spoke of circumcision of the heart. DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Pneumati theou latreuontes" — worship rendered by God's Spirit marks the true covenant community, the heavenly assembly on earth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: though I myself having confidence even in flesh. If any other man thinks to have confidence in flesh, I more:
circumcised the eighth day, of the nation of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, according to the law a Pharisee,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine
verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: according to zeal, persecuting the church, according to righteousness that is in the law, having become blameless.
But whatever things were gain to me, these I have counted as loss on account of Christ.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular neuter

verses.flow: But indeed, even more, I count all things to be loss on account of the surpassing worth of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord, on account of whom I suffered the loss of all things, and I count them as refuse, that I might gain Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Skybala" (σκύβαλα, Strong's G4657) is a rare and viscerally strong term meaning refuse, dung, or filth — used only here in the NT (hapax legomenon). Paul employs this shocking word to express the utter worthlessness of his former credentials compared to knowing Christ. "Hyperechon" (ὑπερέχον) denotes surpassing excellence or supremacy — the knowledge of Christ surpasses all things in worth.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: and be found in him, not having my own righteousness, that from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God upon faith,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Dikaiosynēn tēn ek theou" — the righteousness from God, contrasted with self-achieved righteousness from the law, is the heart of Pauline soteriology. "Dia pisteōs Christou" may be rendered "through faith in Christ" (objective genitive) or "through the faithfulness of Christ" (subjective genitive) — both readings have ancient support. This parallels Rom 3:21-22 and Gal 2:16.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: to know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings, being conformed to his death,
if somehow I might attain to the resurrection from the dead.
verses.note: GREEK: "Exanastasin" (ἐξανάστασιν, Strong's G1815) is a NT hapax legomenon — the double-prefixed form (ek + ana + stasis) intensifies the concept beyond ordinary "anastasis," pointing to the out-resurrection from among the dead, the final eschatological resurrection of the righteous. TYPOLOGY: This ultimate resurrection hope is grounded in Christ's own resurrection as firstfruits (1 Cor 15:20-23).

verses.flow: Not that I have already obtained, or have already been perfected, but I press on, if indeed I may lay hold of that for which also I was laid hold of by Christ.

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine
verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: Brothers, I do not reckon myself to have laid hold; but one thing: forgetting the things behind and reaching forth to the things ahead,
according to the mark I press on toward the prize of the upward calling of God in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Brabeion" (βραβεῖον, Strong's G1017) is the prize awarded to victors in Greek athletic games — used also in 1 Cor 9:24. "Anō klēseōs" (upward calling) denotes the heavenward summons of God. HISTORICAL: The athletic metaphor would resonate powerfully in a Roman colony like Philippi, where Greek games and Roman civic competitions were well known.

verses.flow: As many therefore as are mature, let us think this; and if in anything you think otherwise, even this God shall reveal to you.
Nevertheless, to what we have attained, by the same let us walk.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Become fellow-imitators of me, brothers, and observe those walking thus, even as you have us as a pattern.
verses.note: GREEK: "Symmimētai" (συμμιμηταί, Strong's G4831) is a NT hapax — a compound meaning "fellow-imitators together," not merely individual imitators but a communal imitation of Paul's pattern. "Typon" (τύπον) means pattern, model, or stamp — from which English "type" derives. TYPOLOGY: Paul presents himself as a living pattern of one whose life is being conformed to Christ's death and resurrection (vv.10-11).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: For many walk, of whom I told you often, and now tell you even weeping, that they are the enemies of the cross of Christ,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: whose end is destruction, whose god is the belly, and whose glory is in their shame, who set their minds on earthly things.
For our citizenship exists in the heavens, from which also we eagerly await a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ,
verses.note: GREEK: "Politeuma" (πολίτευμα, Strong's G4175) means citizenship or commonwealth — a politically charged term for residents of Philippi, a Roman colony whose citizens prized their Roman citizenship. HISTORICAL: Philippians held Roman citizenship and lived as a colony of Rome in Macedonia; Paul subverts this identity by declaring believers' true "politeuma" is in the heavens. THEOLOGICAL: The awaited "Sōtēra" (Savior) and "Kyrion" (Lord) are titles the Roman emperor claimed — Paul assigns them exclusively to Jesus Christ.

verses.flow: who shall transform the body of our humiliation, conformed to the body of his glory, according to the working of his being able even to subject all things to himself.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Metaschēmatisei" (μετασχηματίσει) means to transform or transfigure the outward form — the body of humiliation will be made "symmorphon" (conformed, sharing the same form) with Christ's glorified body. This is the culmination of the conformity to Christ begun in suffering (v.10). TYPOLOGY: This transformation fulfills the pattern of Adam's fallen body being restored and surpassed in the last Adam (1 Cor 15:42-49; Rom 8:29). The subjection of all things echoes Ps 8:6 and Ps 110:1, affirming Christ's universal sovereignty.

verses.flow: So then, my brothers, beloved and longed-for, my joy and crown, in this manner stand firm in the Lord, beloved.
verses.note: GREEK: "Crown" (στέφανος, stephanos, Strong's G4735) refers to the victor's wreath, not a royal diadem (diadēma). Paul views the Philippian believers as his eschatological reward, the evidence of his apostolic labor (cf. 1 Thess 2:19). "Longed-for" (ἐπιπόθητοι, epipothētoi) is rare in Greek literature and expresses deep, yearning affection. STRUCTURE: This verse serves as a hinge between the warnings of chapter 3 and the practical exhortations of chapter 4.

verses.flow: Euodia I exhort, and Syntyche I exhort, to think the same thing in the Lord.
Yes, I ask also you, true yokefellow, assist them, who in the gospel labored together with me, with both Clement and the rest of my fellow-workers, whose names are in the book of life.
verses.note: GREEK: "True yokefellow" (γνήσιε σύζυγε, gnēsie syzuge) is debated — σύζυγος could be a proper name "Syzygus" or a description of a genuine companion. "Labored together" (συνήθλησαν, synēthlēsan, Strong's G4866) is an athletic term meaning "contended alongside," evoking the image of the arena. THEOLOGICAL: "Book of life" (βίβλος ζωῆς) draws on OT imagery of God's heavenly register (Ex 32:32; Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1), carried forward into Revelation (Rev 3:5; 20:12).

verses.flow: Rejoice in the Lord always; again I will say, rejoice!
Let your gentleness be known to all men. The Lord is near.
verses.note: GREEK: "Gentleness" (ἐπιεικές, epieikes, Strong's G1933) denotes a yielding, magnanimous reasonableness that does not insist on strict legal rights — difficult to render with a single English word. THEOLOGICAL: "The Lord is near" (ὁ κύριος ἐγγύς) carries a dual sense: the Lord's spatial nearness in presence and his temporal nearness in return (parousia). This echoes the Aramaic prayer "Maranatha" (1 Cor 16:22) and grounds the call to gentleness in eschatological expectation.

verses.flow: Be anxious about nothing, but in everything, by prayer and by supplication, with thanksgiving, let your requests be made known to God.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine

verses.flow: And the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, shall guard your hearts and your thoughts in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: GREEK: "Surpasses" (ὑπερέχουσα, hyperechousa, Strong's G5242) means "rising above, excelling beyond." "Guard" (φρουρήσει, phrourēsei, Strong's G5432) is a military term meaning to garrison or stand sentinel — God's peace acts as a divine sentinel over the believer's inner life. THEOLOGICAL: The peace here is not subjective tranquility but the objective shalom of God himself — his own cosmic peace — standing watch over hearts and minds. The locus of this guarding is "in Christ Jesus," the covenantal sphere of divine protection.

verses.flow: Finally, brothers, whatever things are true, whatever things are honorable, whatever things are righteous, whatever things are pure, whatever things are lovely, whatever things are of good report — if there is any virtue, and if there is any praise — reckon upon these things.
verses.note: GREEK: This verse employs a rhetorical device (anaphora) with the sixfold repetition of ὅσα (hosa, "whatever things"). Several terms here overlap with Greco-Roman virtue lists: σεμνά (semna, "honorable/dignified"), ἀρετή (aretē, "virtue," Strong's G703) — notably the only use of aretē by Paul, a term central to Greek philosophical ethics. HISTORICAL: Paul engages Hellenistic moral vocabulary familiar to the Philippian audience, demonstrating that genuine virtue, wherever found, is subsumed under and measured by the gospel.

verses.flow: What things you both learned and received and heard and saw in me, these things practice; and the God of peace shall be with you.
And I rejoiced in the Lord greatly, that now at last you have revived your thinking on behalf of me — concerning which you were indeed thinking, but you lacked opportunity.
Not that I speak according to want, for I have learned, in whatever circumstances I am, to be content.
I know both how to be brought low, and I know how to abound. In everything and in all things I have been initiated, both to be filled and to hunger, both to abound and to lack.
verses.note: GREEK: "I have been initiated" (μεμύημαι, memyēmai, Strong's G3453) is a perfect passive from myeō, a technical term from the Greek mystery religions meaning to be initiated into secret rites. Paul co-opts this language: his "mystery initiation" is learning contentment through Christ in all circumstances. HISTORICAL: This language would have resonated powerfully in the Greco-Roman world of Philippi, a Roman colony where mystery cults were prevalent.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular masculine

verses.flow: I am able to do all things in the one who strengthens me.
verses.note: GREEK: "I am able to do all things" (πάντα ἰσχύω, panta ischyō) — ἰσχύω (Strong's G2480) denotes having strength or power. "The one who strengthens me" (τῷ ἐνδυναμοῦντί με, tō endynamounti me) uses ἐνδυναμόω (Strong's G1743), "to empower, pour strength into." THEOLOGICAL: The "all things" must be read in context — not unlimited power for any desire, but the ability to endure all circumstances of abundance and want (vv. 11-12). The source of strength is Christ, not the self — a direct counter to Stoic self-sufficiency.

words.m: verb aorist active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Nevertheless, you did well, having shared together in my affliction.
And you yourselves know also, Philippians, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no assembly shared with me in the matter of giving and receiving, except you alone.
For even in Thessalonica, both once and twice, you sent for my need.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative singular masculine

verses.flow: Not that I seek the gift, but I seek the fruit that abounds to your account.

words.m: verb aorist middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: But I have received all things in full and abound; I have been filled, having received from Epaphroditus the things from you — a fragrance of sweet aroma, a sacrifice acceptable, well-pleasing to God.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "A fragrance of sweet aroma" (ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας, osmēn euōdias) directly echoes LXX sacrificial language (Gen 8:21; Ex 29:18; Lev 1:9). Paul frames the Philippians' financial gift as a priestly offering ascending to God. "Acceptable" (δεκτήν) and "well-pleasing" (εὐάρεστον) are cultic terms applied to sacrifices God receives with favor. THEOLOGICAL: This transforms ordinary material generosity into liturgical worship, collapsing the sacred-secular divide.

verses.flow: And my God shall fill your every need according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This is a promissory declaration, not a wish — the future indicative πληρώσει ("shall fill") expresses divine certainty. God's supply is measured "according to" (κατά) his riches, not "out of" — the standard of giving matches the inexhaustible measure of divine glory. The phrase "in glory in Christ Jesus" locates both the riches and the fulfillment within the glorified reign of the Messiah.

verses.flow: Now to our God and Father be the glory unto the ages of the ages. Amen.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: "Unto the ages of the ages" (εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας τῶν αἰώνων) is the strongest Greek expression for unending eternity, a Hebraism of superlative intensification (cf. Rev 1:6; Gal 1:5). THEOLOGICAL: The doxology ascribes glory to "our God and Father" — the intimate covenant language of divine fatherhood grounding all of Paul's theology of provision and care in this passage.

verses.flow: Greet every saint in Christ Jesus. The brothers with me greet you.
All the saints greet you, and especially those of the household of Caesar.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Those of the household of Caesar" (οἱ ἐκ τῆς Καίσαρος οἰκίας) refers not to the imperial family but to the extensive network of slaves, freedmen, and officials in Caesar's administrative household — the familia Caesaris. Paul writes from imprisonment (Phil 1:13), and the gospel has penetrated even into the heart of Roman imperial power. This is a stunning testament to the advance of the gospel into the structures of the empire itself.

verses.flow: The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit.
